input.content:
Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 20. The new upper round bound is round 25; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

Additional instructions for this continuation:
The previous pass did not reach Author/Critic agreement. Continue the existing run up to round 25; focus on resolving the remaining Critic objections instead of restarting.

### Round 25 of 25 ###
Budget used so far: $529.11 / $1000.00

### First Proof LaTeX contract ###
First Proof LaTeX submission contract:
- `answer.tex` must be a complete standalone LaTeX document.
- Use exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- The compiled PDF must be at most 12 pages.
- The `fullpage` package is permitted.
- Do not use any other margin/layout changes: no `geometry`, `a4wide`,
  `typearea`, `anysize`, manual `\textwidth`, `\textheight`,
  `\oddsidemargin`, `\evensidemargin`, `\topmargin`, etc.
- Do not change line spacing: no `setspace`, `\linespread`,
  `\baselinestretch`, `\onehalfspacing`, or `\doublespacing`.
- Do not change font size inside the document: no `\small`,
  `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize`.
- Run `pdflatex` and fix compile errors before claiming readiness.


### Workspace files ###
- answer.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-RRDVzhQxR3JAzUT1vDi85N-answer.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/answer.tex`.
- research_notes.tex: read from `/mnt/data/file-Wbd3KeMxqRn2BgTj2jTCWC-research_notes.tex` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/research_notes.tex`.
- references.bib: read from `/mnt/data/file-Dpqq7aQWtRMF1U5ou2xboA-references.bib` (read-only); write your edited version to `/mnt/data/references.bib`.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### LaTeX contract

I compiled the revised `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`. It compiles successfully, with no unresolved references or LaTeX errors, and the resulting PDF has 6 pages. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, the only layout package is `fullpage`, and I found no forbidden line-spacing, margin, or in-document font-size changes. Thus there is no LaTeX-contract obstruction.

### Literature/source checks

The revised proof’s broad references are real and relevant. DKEP Section 2.3 indeed describes the normal bidual via a support projection \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) and states that for an invariant \(C^*\)-subalgebra \(B\subset A\), the support \(q_B\) commutes with \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) and gives a nonunital corner embedding of \((B^\sharp)^*\). DKEP Proposition 2.10 then records the simultaneous-normal version. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_B\) as the closure of the span of \(x_1Jy_1JTJy_2Jx_2\), \(T\in e_B\mathbb B(L^2M)e_B\), and identifies it as the hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP defines \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\), proves \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), and Theorem 5.10 gives the cyclic-vector criterion for relative mixing. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Example 5.11 defines \(X_H\) from operators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) and says that for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence this recovers the subalgebra boundary of Example 3.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 support the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule, while CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 supports the final full-factor claim for join-irreducible graphs with at least three vertices. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

### Status of previous concerns

Several earlier issues are improved:

1. **LaTeX compliance** is now confirmed.
2. **Support bookkeeping** is much better grounded: the revised proof now closely matches DKEP’s Section 2.3 support-corner construction and Proposition 2.10.
3. **The finite-boundary compression lemma** is clearer, especially the normality domination argument and the use of DKEP Lemma 8.5’s u.c.p. map. DKEP Lemma 8.5 does give the bidual criterion and the relevant natural maps into the compact normal corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
4. **The Bass–Serre reduced-word argument** is more explicit and is plausibly correct.
5. **The graph-product final application** is mostly correct conditional on the technical lemmas.

However, a new fatal problem appears in the revised proof, and it affects the core intersection argument.

---

## Fatal issue: the subalgebra-fusion lemma contains a false coefficient computation

In Lemma `subalgebra fusion`, the proof asserts:

\[
\text{“the positive coefficients of the vectors }x\zeta_By\text{ are }
xJyJ\,e_B\,Jy^*Jx^*.” 
\]

With DKEP’s convention \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\), this formula is not correct in general. DKEP explicitly defines \(T_\xi\) by \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

A simple check already disproves the displayed formula. Take \(B=\mathbb C\subset N\), with \(N\) a noncommutative finite factor, and take \(x,y\in\mathcal U(N)\). Then

\[
\mathcal H_B=L^2N\otimes L^2N,\qquad \zeta_B=1\otimes 1.
\]

For \(\xi=x\zeta_By=x\otimes y\), a direct computation gives

\[
T_\xi(\widehat b)
 = L_\xi^*R_\xi(\widehat b)
 = \tau(x^*bx)\widehat{y^*y}
 = \tau(b)\widehat 1,
\]

so \(T_\xi=e_{\mathbb C}\), hence \(T_\xi^*T_\xi=e_{\mathbb C}\). But the formula in the proof gives

\[
xJyJ\,e_{\mathbb C}\,Jy^*Jx^*,
\]

which is the rank-one projection onto the vector \(xy^*\) up to the usual identification. For \(xy^*\notin\mathbb C1\), this is not \(e_{\mathbb C}\). Thus the asserted coefficient identity is false.

This is not cosmetic. The proof uses that false identity to conclude that the hereditary algebra generated by positive coefficients of \(\mathcal H_B\) contains all generators \(xJyJe_B\) of \(X_B\). Without that step, the first assertion of Lemma `subalgebra fusion` is unproved.

### Consequences of this gap

The false coefficient computation propagates through the proof:

- Lemma `finite fusion calculus` uses Lemma `subalgebra fusion` to assert that \(X_{M_U}\) has an approximate unit built from positive coefficients of \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).
- Lemma `intersection of boundary supports` depends on those special approximate units.
- The final passage from relative proper proximality for the link boundaries \(X_{M_{L_v}}\) to proper proximality depends on Lemma `intersection`.

So the main technical bridge of the proof is still not rigorously established.

A correct route may exist using DKEP Example 5.11 and a linking/coefficient argument with \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), but the proof currently written does not provide it. DKEP Example 5.11 identifies \(X_H\) for \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) with the subalgebra boundary, but that is not the same as the false formula for positive coefficients of \(x\zeta_By\) in \(H\otimes_B\bar H\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

## Section-by-section audit

### Problem interpretation

The interpretation of irreducible as nontrivial join-irreducibility is appropriate, and the complement-connected equivalence is standard for finite simple graphs. The draft explicitly records this interpretation, satisfying the ambiguity requirement.

### Support bookkeeping lemma

This is much improved. The one-normal support statement is supported by DKEP Section 2.3, and the simultaneous-normal statement is plausibly obtained using DKEP Proposition 2.10. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) I no longer regard this as the primary fatal defect.

The equality \(pr_X=pr_{K_X(N)}=q_X\) is still compressed into a short annihilator argument, but it is plausible given DKEP Proposition 2.4 and Proposition 3.6. I would prefer more detail, but this is not the decisive obstruction.

### Lemma `subalgebra fusion`

This is the main failure. The proof’s central coefficient formula for \(x\zeta_By\) is false, as explained above. Since this lemma is used to build approximate units for \(X_{M_U}\), the rest of the proof cannot be certified.

The later use of DKEP Theorem 5.10 in the same lemma is plausible, but it does not repair the first assertion.

### Lemma `finite fusion calculus`

The shift from the previous coefficient \(*\)-algebra to a \(T_\xi\)-operator algebra is an improvement. DKEP indeed gives \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) and \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The claim that \(\mathcal T_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra is now much more credible.

However, the approximate-unit conclusion depends directly on the faulty Lemma `subalgebra fusion`. Therefore this lemma is not established as written.

### Lemma `intersection of boundary supports`

The use of CDHJKN’s fusion rule is appropriate in spirit. CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 decomposes the relevant graph-product bimodules, and Proposition 5.5 gives the fusion rule over intersections. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

But the proof relies on approximate units from the positive cones supplied by Lemma `finite fusion calculus`, which in turn relies on the false coefficient-generation argument. Thus the crucial inequality

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}
\]

remains unproved in the submitted draft.

### Finite-boundary-to-compact-boundary lemma

This part is substantially improved and is likely correct conditional on the support framework. The compression argument, the GNS projection argument, and the use of the compact-corner u.c.p. map are now clearly stated. DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the bidual central-state criterion used here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### Bass–Serre proposition

This section is also improved. The reduced-word commutator formulas and paradoxical inequalities are plausible. The invocation of DKEP Lemma 6.1 is now better justified; DKEP Lemma 6.1 does allow checking the commutator condition on an ultraweakly dense generating \(*\)-subalgebra. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) I do not see a fatal issue here.

### Graph-product application

Conditional on the previous lemmas, the graph-product application is mostly correct. The AFP decomposition is standard in graph products, the choice of \(w_1,w_2\) is combinatorially sound, and CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 supports the diffuse full factor conclusion for join-irreducible graphs with \(|V|\ge3\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The final step still fails because the boundary-support intersection lemma is not proved.

---

## Overall verdict

The revision fixes several formal and expository issues, and the global strategy remains plausible. But `answer.tex` still does **not** constitute a complete rigorous proof. The revised Lemma `subalgebra fusion` contains a false coefficient identity, and that lemma is essential for constructing the approximate units used in the boundary-support intersection argument. Consequently the proof of the central inequality \(q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}\) is not valid as written.

### Workflow compile/format feedback ###
No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

### Advisory Council replies (if you requested any last turn) ###
(empty)

### Compute worker reply (if you commissioned one last turn) ###
(empty)

Edit the canonical files via code_interpreter cells as described in
the system prompt. Optionally invoke ``<council>`` on at most one
specific sub-question, and/or ``<compute_agent>`` on at most one
focused computation/code/literature task, or set
``<ready>true</ready>`` if you believe the answer is ready for
submission. Do NOT paste the canonical files in your reply.

summary.text:
**Updating documents and verifying details**

I need to update answer.tex, which is already 6 pages, and revise it to possibly 12 pages. I also want to correct research_notes and maybe append a "Coefficient correction" note. I’m pondering if I should leave references.bib unchanged or copy it to canonical. I guess I should write everything necessary and evaluate if it's ready or if there are gaps. I want to ensure that my final proof has no "Remaining open issues" and check coefficients carefully for consistency.

**Clarifying mathematical relationships**

Equation (1) outlines a foundational relationship: if ξ is in M-N and η is in N-P, then T_{ξ⊗_Nη}=T_η T_ξ, and T_{\barξ}=T_ξ^*. For H={}_N L^2N_B, both ξ and η belong to H. Then \bar ξ becomes B-N, and \(η⊗_B \bar ξ\) stays in N-N. If DKEP Example X_H is generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), then s=T_{η⊗\bar ξ}. It all leads to insights about positive coefficients in bounded algebraic tensors, tying together key concepts effectively.

**Understanding hereditary algebras**

I need to clarify that \(X_B\) is the hereditary subalgebra generated by S, which means it's the smallest hereditary C*-subalgebra containing S. However, arbitrary non-selfadjoint S might not fit into this hereditary framework. If it contains an element, it must also support it. DKEP defines the hereditary C*-algebra generated by specific operators, so it's confirmed that S⊂X_B. Since \(T_α^*T_α\) is in X_B, Y must also be included there. I need to tread carefully with "hereditarily generated" terms as it may not mean they contain the operators but rather are generated by them as a C*-algebra.

**Patching the answer**

I need to confirm that \cT_U includes all bounded vectors in finite direct sum fusions, which also covers non-empty H_U. Since algebraic tensors are bounded vectors in H_U, their corresponding T belongs to \cT_U. That’s a good start! Now, I'll focus on patching the answer using a Python string replace lemma. Before I dive into the notes, I should first work on the answer and plan a targeted replace starting from "For an expected inclusion..." through the lemma subalgebra. I have to be careful not to overshoot the page limit as I draft this.

output.code:
from pathlib import Path
ans_path=Path("/mnt/data/file-RRDVzhQxR3JAzUT1vDi85N-answer.tex")
ans=ans_path.read_text()
start=ans.index(r"For an expected inclusion")
end=ans.index(r"\begin{lemma}[finite fusion calculus]")
old=ans[start:end]
print(old)

new = r"""For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), put \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and
\[
        \cH_B=H\otimes_B\overline H
             ={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
Let \(\mathcal E_B\) be the linear span of
\(T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}\), where \(\xi,\eta\) are bounded vectors in \(H\).

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra fusion]\label{lem:subalg}
The boundary piece \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by the positive
operators \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\), where \(\alpha\) runs over bounded finite
sums of elementary tensors in \(\cH_B\).  Moreover
\(\cH_B\cong\overline{\cH_B}\), and every bounded coefficient of every direct
sum of copies of \(\cH_B\) is in \(K_{X_B}(N)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Example 5.11 associates to \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) the hereditary algebra
generated by the operators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\), and
states that this algebra is exactly the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\).  By (1),
\[
        T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta,      \tag{2}
\]
so \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by \(\mathcal E_B\).  Let \(Y\) be the
hereditary algebra generated by the positive operators in the first sentence
of the lemma.  If \(s=T_{\eta\otimes\bar\xi}\in\mathcal E_B\), then
\(s^*s=T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) for \(\alpha=\eta\otimes\bar\xi\), while
\(ss^*=T_\beta^*T_\beta\) for \(\beta=\xi\otimes\bar\eta\), using
\(T_{\bar\alpha}=T_\alpha^*\).  Hence \(s\in Y\).  Thus \(X_B\subset Y\).
The reverse inclusion follows from (2), because \(T_\alpha\in\mathcal E_B\)
for every bounded finite elementary sum \(\alpha\).  Therefore \(X_B=Y\).

The map \(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta}\mapsto\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi\) extends
to an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence isomorphism
\(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\).  Also, (2) says that the coefficients of a
cyclic set of bounded elementary tensors for \(\cH_B\) lie in \(X_B\).  The
cyclic-vector criterion of DKEP Theorem 5.10 therefore makes \(\cH_B\) mixing
relative to \(X_B\times X_B\).  Hence, by DKEP Proposition 5.9, bounded
coefficients \(T_\alpha^*T_\beta\) of \(\cH_B\) lie in \(K_{X_B}(N)\).  For a
direct sum of copies of \(\cH_B\) we use the union of these cyclic sets and
the same criterion.
\end{proof}

"""
ans2=ans[:start]+new+ans[end:]
# Need update equation numbering: new (2) conflicts with intersection displayed tag (2). We used tag{2}, existing later has tag{2}, duplicate.
# Let's use tag{F} or no tag? Remove tag.
ans2=ans2.replace(r"      \tag{2}", "")
Path("/mnt/data/answer.tex").write_text(ans2)
print(ans2[start:start+3000])

p=Path("/mnt/data/answer.tex")
text=p.read_text()
text=text.replace("The reverse inclusion follows from (2), because", "The reverse inclusion follows from the displayed identity, because")
text=text.replace("Also, (2) says that", "Also, the displayed identity says that")
p.write_text(text)
print("undefined (2)?", "(2)" in text[text.index("DKEP Example 5.11"):text.index(r"\begin{lemma}[finite fusion")])

p=Path("/mnt/data/answer.tex")
text=p.read_text()
old = r"""The algebra property of \(\cT_U\) is now just (1):
\(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\), and
\(T_\xi^*=T_{\bar\xi}\); direct sums absorb finite sums.  By Lemma
\ref{lem:subalg}, \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by the positive
coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) of \(H_U\), which are among the \(t^*t\)'s.
For a finite positive sum \(s\) of such generators,
"""
new2 = r"""The algebra property of \(\cT_U\) is now just (1):
\(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\), and
\(T_\xi^*=T_{\bar\xi}\); direct sums absorb finite sums.  By Lemma
\ref{lem:subalg}, \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated by the positive
operators \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) coming from bounded finite elementary vectors in
\(H_U\), and the corresponding \(T_\xi\)'s are elements of \(\cT_U\).  For a
finite positive sum \(s\) of such generators,
"""
if old not in text:
    print("old not found")
else:
    text=text.replace(old,new2)
p.write_text(text)
print(text[text.index("The algebra property"):text.index("If \\(Z\\subset U\\)")])

import subprocess, os, textwrap, pathlib, json, sys, re
res=subprocess.run(["pdflatex","-interaction=nonstopmode","answer.tex"], cwd="/mnt/data", capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=120)
print(res.returncode)
print(res.stdout[-2000:])
print(res.stderr[-1000:])
# run twice
res2=subprocess.run(["pdflatex","-interaction=nonstopmode","answer.tex"], cwd="/mnt/data", capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=120)
print("2",res2.returncode)
print(res2.stdout[-1000:])

summary.text:
**Checking for warnings**

I need to determine if there are any warnings after the second operation. It seems like there might not be any, since the standard output tail doesn’t show anything concerning. I’ll take a look at the log file to search for warnings by using grep. This way, I can ensure everything is working smoothly without missing any important details. It’s always good to double-check!

**Exploring tags and counts**

text=p.read_text()

                  r"        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .                                  \tag{2}")

notes_path=Path("/mnt/data/file-Wbd3KeMxqRn2BgTj2jTCWC-research_notes.tex")
notes=notes_path.read_text()
append = r"""

\section*{Coefficient generation for subalgebra boundaries}

For an expected inclusion $B\subset N$, the safe way to generate the
subalgebra boundary is through DKEP Example 5.11, not through an attempted
closed formula for positive coefficients of translated Jones vectors.  Let
$H={}_NL^2N_B$ and $\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_B\overline H$.  Example 5.11 gives
\[
  X_B=\operatorname{her}\{T_\xi^*T_\eta:\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\}.
\]
Using the fusion identity,
\[
  T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta,
\]
so the generators of $X_B$ are ordinary coefficients $T_\alpha$ of
$\mathcal H_B$ for bounded elementary tensors $\alpha$.  If $Y$ is the
hereditary algebra generated by $T_\alpha^*T_\alpha$ for bounded finite
elementary sums $\alpha\in\mathcal H_B$, then every generator
$s=T_{\eta\otimes\bar\xi}$ of $X_B$ satisfies $s^*s\in Y$ and
$ss^*\in Y$ (the latter is the positive coefficient of
$\xi\otimes\bar\eta$), hence $s\in Y$.  Conversely the positive generators
belong to $X_B$.  Therefore $X_B=Y$.

This avoids the tempting but generally false identity
$T_{x\zeta_By}^*T_{x\zeta_By}=xJyJe_BJy^*Jx^*$ for
$\zeta_B=1\otimes_B1$.  For example, when $B=\mathbb C$ and $x,y$ are
unitaries in a noncommutative finite factor, the actual positive coefficient
of $x\otimes y$ is $e_{\mathbb C}$, while the displayed rank-one projection is
usually different.
"""
if notes.rstrip().endswith(r"\end{document}"):
    notes2=notes.rstrip()[:-len(r"\end{document}")]+append+"\n\\end{document}\n"
else:
    notes2=notes+append
Path("/mnt/data/research_notes.tex").write_text(notes2)
print(Path("/mnt/data/research_notes.tex").read_text()[-3000:])

I need to replace the old paragraph with the correct summary. I think it would be better to avoid any duplicate text, but maybe it's okay to have it in some cases, as long as it’s accurate. So, I’ll go ahead and replace the old paragraph starting from "For a subalgebra boundary..." and make sure the appended duplicate stays in place. That seems like a solid plan!

output.code:
p=Path("/mnt/data/research_notes.tex")
s=p.read_text()
start=s.index("For a subalgebra boundary \\(X_B\\), Example 3.4")
end=s.index("For the boundary-support intersection", start)
replacement = r"""For a subalgebra boundary \(X_B\), the reliable coefficient-generation
argument uses DKEP Example 5.11.  With \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and
\(\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_B\overline H\),
\[
  T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta .
\]
Example 5.11 identifies \(X_B\) with the hereditary algebra generated by
these operators.  Therefore \(X_B\) is also hereditarily generated by the
positive operators \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\), where \(\alpha\) ranges over
bounded finite elementary sums in \(\mathcal H_B\): if
\(s=T_{\eta\otimes\bar\xi}\), then both \(s^*s\) and \(ss^*\) are of this
positive form, and a hereditary algebra containing both contains \(s\).

"""
s2=s[:start]+replacement+s[end:]
p.write_text(s2)
print(p.read_text()[69500:71200])
print("false phrase present", "positive coefficients of the translates" in p.read_text())

**Considering research notes**

I’m thinking about whether I need to compile my research notes. It’s not strictly necessary, but I should check for any syntax issues. I could compile them if needed, but it’s not urgent. Maybe I’ll decide later if it’s worth doing!

output.code:
p=Path("/mnt/data/answer.tex")
text=p.read_text()
old = r"""The map \(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta}\mapsto\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi\) extends
to an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence isomorphism
\(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\).  Also, the displayed identity says that the coefficients of a
cyclic set of bounded elementary tensors for \(\cH_B\) lie in \(X_B\).  The
cyclic-vector criterion of DKEP Theorem 5.10 therefore makes \(\cH_B\) mixing
relative to \(X_B\times X_B\).  Hence, by DKEP Proposition 5.9, bounded
coefficients \(T_\alpha^*T_\beta\) of \(\cH_B\) lie in \(K_{X_B}(N)\).  For a
direct sum of copies of \(\cH_B\) we use the union of these cyclic sets and
the same criterion.
"""
new = r"""The map \(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta}\mapsto\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi\) extends
to an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence isomorphism
\(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\).  Also, the displayed identity says that the
coefficient operators of a cyclic set of bounded elementary tensors for
\(\cH_B\) lie in \(X_B\).  The cyclic-vector criterion of DKEP Theorem 5.10
therefore makes \(\cH_B\) mixing relative to \(X_B\times X_B\).  Thus every
bounded vector \(\alpha\) in \(\cH_B\) has
\(T_\alpha\in K_{X_B,X_B}(N,N)\).  DKEP Proposition 5.9 gives
\(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha,T_\alpha T_\alpha^*\in K_{X_B}(N)\), and heredity then
gives \(T_\alpha\in K_{X_B}(N)\).  Direct sums are handled by taking the union
of the cyclic sets in the summands.
"""
if old not in text:
    print("old not found")
else:
    text=text.replace(old,new)
p.write_text(text)
# compile twice
for i in range(2):
    res=subprocess.run(["pdflatex","-interaction=nonstopmode","answer.tex"], cwd="/mnt/data", capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=120)
    print(i,res.returncode,res.stdout[-300:])
log=Path("/mnt/data/answer.log").read_text(errors='ignore')
for line in log.splitlines():
    if "Warning" in line or "undefined" in line.lower():
        print(line)
print("pages? check pdfinfo")
res=subprocess.run(["pdfinfo","/mnt/data/answer.pdf"], capture_output=True, text=True)
print([line for line in res.stdout.splitlines() if line.startswith("Pages")])

content.text:
Updated `answer.tex` and `research_notes.tex`. The false translated-Jones-vector coefficient computation has been removed and replaced by the DKEP Example 5.11 / fusion-identity argument using \(T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*T_\eta\). I also rechecked the downstream approximate-unit argument accordingly.

`pdflatex` was run twice on `answer.tex`; it compiles successfully with no warnings, and the PDF is 6 pages.

<ready>true</ready>
